selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm eighty-one years old and I don't mess around with social media, and honestly, I think that's saved me a lot of headaches. My kids are always showing me things on their phones, worried about this story or that video, and half the time it turns out to be complete nonsense. From what I've seen working at the restaurant all these years, people believe what they want to believe, and these social media companies know exactly how to exploit that. I came to New York decades ago as an immigrant, and back then if you wanted news, you read the newspaper or watched the evening broadcast. Sure, there was bias, but at least there were editors and some standards. Now anyone with a phone can spread whatever garbage they want, and it spreads faster than a kitchen fire. My children tell me I should get on Facebook or WhatsApp to stay connected, but why would I want that stress? I already worry enough about things without having to figure out what's real and what's fake on a screen. The way I see it, if something important happens, someone will tell me face to face. That's how information should travel, between real people who can look each other in the eye. When customers at work start talking about some wild story they saw online, I just nod and keep pouring coffee. I've learned not to argue too much because people get defensive about what they've seen on their feeds. They act like questioning it is questioning them personally. The problem isn't just the fake content itself, it's that people have stopped trusting their own common sense. They see something outrageous and instead of thinking it through, they share it immediately. If you don't have social media like me, you're already managing it the best way possible. For those who do use it, I'd say trust your instincts, talk to real people, and remember that not everything deserves your attention or worry. Life's too short to spend it sorting through digital lies when you could be doing something worthwhile instead.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 81-year-old immigrant man working part-time as a waiter in New York with no social media presence. His lack of social media engagement is the most critical factor shaping his perspective. His moderate neuroticism suggests he experiences worry and stress, which would make him view social media's chaotic information environment negatively. His low agreeableness indicates he's likely skeptical, direct, and not particularly concerned with being diplomatic. His moderate extraversion means he values real-world social connections over digital ones. His age and immigrant background give him a comparative perspective between pre-digital and digital eras. His working-class occupation and vocational education suggest practical, street-smart wisdom rather than academic analysis. His Islamic faith and family-oriented life (married with 3 children) provide stability and traditional values. The tone should be direct, somewhat skeptical, practical, and grounded in real-world experience rather than technical knowledge.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, skeptical, practical, slightly gruff but not hostile, drawing from life experience and common sense rather than technical expertise, with undertones of worry about modern developments
